## "The Thinking Machine" (Artificial Intelligence in the 1960s)

### What Is This Video/Film?

**"The Thinking Machine"** was a 53-minute black-and-white television documentary that aired on **October 26, 1960**, as part of the CBS series **"Tomorrow."** It was produced by **CBS News in conjunction with MIT**. The program was designed to introduce the general public to the emerging field of artificial intelligence — only four years after the term "Artificial Intelligence" was coined at the 1956 Dartmouth Conference.

The show was hosted by actor **David Wayne** (known for *Adam's Rib*, *The Twilight Zone*), who co-hosted alongside **Jerome B. Wiesner**, then Director of MIT's Research Laboratory of Electronics (and later MIT President). The program featured demonstrations of computers doing handwriting recognition, playing checkers, and even generating Western movie scripts.

---

### Scientists Interviewed in the Film

| Scientist | Affiliation | Role |
|-----------|-------------|------|
| **Jerome B. Wiesner** | MIT, Director of Research Lab of Electronics | Co-host, primary expert explaining AI |
| **Claude Shannon** | Bell Labs | AI pioneer, attended 1956 Dartmouth Conference |
| **Oliver Selfridge** | MIT | AI pioneer, "Father of Machine Perception," also attended Dartmouth |
| **Arthur Samuel** | IBM | Developer of checkers-playing program, coined "machine learning" (1959) |
| **Herbert Simon** | Carnegie Tech (now CMU) | AI and cognitive psychology pioneer |
| **Allen Newell** | Carnegie Tech (now CMU) | AI pioneer, co-creator of Logic Theorist |
| **Doug Ross** | MIT | Developer of computer-generated playwriting program |
| **Jerome Lettvin** | MIT | Neuroscientist studying frog vision |
| **Jean Piaget** | Geneva Laboratory | Developmental psychologist |
| **Harrison Morse** | MIT | Worked on TX-0 computer script generation |

---

### The Key Question: Which Scientist Predicted Thinking Machines Would Come Sooner?

The answer is **Claude Shannon**, and his prediction stands out dramatically against the others:

#### Claude Shannon (the most optimistic — by far)

> **"I confidently expect that within a matter of 10 or 15 years something will emerge from the laboratories which is not too far from the robot of science fiction fame."**

Shannon outlined a vision of robots with three components:
1. **Sense organs** (like eyes/ears) to perceive the environment
2. A **large general-purpose computer** capable of learning
3. **Output devices** (like hands) to affect the environment

He predicted this would happen by roughly **1970-1975**.

#### Oliver Selfridge (optimistic but longer timeline)

> **"Machines can't write good poetry or produce deathless music yet, but I don't see any stopping block in a line of progress which will enable them to in the long run. I'm convinced that machines can and will think... I am confident that in our lifetime, machines can and will learn to think."**

Selfridge's prediction was framed around "our lifetime" — much longer than Shannon's 10-15 years.

#### The Skeptic (possibly Wiesner or another scientist)

> **"I don't believe that we can say yet that machines do think. I have a basic question which I always ask. And that is, are these producing anything really new? Until I see a machine producing genuinely new things, I will not agree that machines think."**

This scientist was not convinced machines would think at all in the foreseeable future.
